Optics / photonics tape is starting to look structurally broken. 
 
$AAOI, $CIEN, $LITE- all showing wide, sloppy price action with no real follow-through. 
 
During the prior trend, these names consistently rode rising EMAs with clean momentum and controlled pullbacks. That structure is gone. 
 
Now we’re seeing failed breakouts, loss of key levels, and rallies that immediately fade. 
 
$CIEN, which was the most orderly leader from March–May in my view, has already rolled over and lost structure. 
 
This is no longer a “buy dips in trend” environment for the group - it’s transition phase behavior.

$NOK just dropped a meaningful structural signal for the optical / AI infra chain. 
 
A 10x expansion of its photonic chip facility in Allentown, PA with capacity targeted online by Q3 2026 isn’t just incremental—it’s a step-function in US-based optical manufacturing exposure. With <2% of advanced semiconductor test/packaging currently onshore, this puts Nokia into a very small strategic cohort tied directly to AI datacenter buildouts. 
 
Yet the market is still valuing it like a legacy telecom name at ~3.4x forward revenue, while peers like $CIEN (~11.0x) and $COHR (~11.6x) are already priced as AI optical beneficiaries serving the same end customers. 
 
The disconnect is largely mix-based—optical is still a smaller % of $NOK revenue-but capacity expansion + SOTP logic starts to re-rate that mix over time. 
 
This is how optical exposure gets repriced—quietly, then all at once.
$CIEN what's this about down $30?

$CIEN just posted a strong quarter  EPS beat by 12%, revenue up ~40% YoY, and full-year guidance raised to $6.3B. 
 
Yet the stock is still ~30% off its highs, which honestly feels a bit disconnected from the numbers. 
 
Key points: 
EPS +12% beat 
Revenue +40% YoY 
FY guide raised to $6.3B 
~$7.7B backlog 
Stock ~30% below peak 
 
What stands out is this wasn’t really a demand problem. 
 
After a huge prior run, even solid “beat and raise” prints near consensus can trigger profit-taking. Then you had the ~$2.875B convertible deal adding extra mechanical pressure on top. 
 
But the bigger picture hasn’t changed. 
 
Hyperscalers are still scaling AI clusters across regions, and coherent optical networks (800G/1.6T) are becoming the backbone of that buildout  exactly where $CIEN sits as a global leader in data center interconnect. 
 
So this feels less like a broken trend, and more like a reset after a big move inside a still-expanding cycle.

$CIEN just printed a classic “sell the reaction, not the business” setup. 
 
EPS beat by ~12%, revenue up ~40% YoY, and full-year guidance raised to ~$6.3B — essentially in-line with Street expectations at ~$6.29B. After a massive prior run, that type of print often triggers mechanical profit-taking rather than continuation. 
 
Then you had the $2.875B convertible issuance adding short-term hedging pressure into the June 11 close, amplifying downside flow regardless of fundamentals. 
 
But zoom out: management still signals strong visibility well beyond historical norms. Meanwhile hyperscalers are building cross-region GPU clusters. 
 
That’s the core of what $CIEN dominates globally in Data Center Interconnect. With ~$7.7B backlog and a ~$50B TAM expanding into 2029, this is a scale infrastructure story, not a single print story. 
 
Stock is ~30% off highs with catalysts largely absorbed. Now it’s about whether AI infra spending continues to accelerate.

Many investors focus only on GPUs, but Jensen Huang has repeatedly pointed out that the real AI bottleneck lies across the broader infrastructure stack. As AI evolves from Generative AI to Agentic AI and eventually Physical AI, networking, optical communications, and test & measurement solutions become increasingly critical. 
 
VIAV, NOK, MKSI, and CIEN are all beneficiaries of AI-driven data traffic growth and network upgrades. They may not get the same attention as NVDA, but they play essential roles in enabling AI at scale. The market narrative is expanding from pure compute to connectivity and validation infrastructure. 
 
The question is: will the next AI leg continue rotating into these infrastructure names, or will capital flow back to core leaders like NVDA? Which name are you most bullish on?

Optics supply chain check  
 
Latest earnings calls are telling a very consistent story across the board - demand is still outrunning supply at nearly every major node. 
 
$LITE: effectively sold out, >30% demand not being shipped, capacity locked via LTAs through 2027 
$AAOI: scaling hard (100k → 930k units/month by 2027) yet still expects demand > supply into mid-2027 
$COHR: bookings into 2028, doubling InP capacity twice in 18 months, calls out industry-wide bottleneck 
$CIEN: backlog up to $7.7B (+$2.6B in 6 months) 
$CSCO: hyperscaler AI orders revised from $5B → $9B mid-year 
 
Put it together and the 800G / 1.6T cycle still looks structurally undersupplied (~27–30%). 
 
If CPO slips to 2028, this entire stack effectively becomes the backbone of AI buildout for another 2 years.

$CIEN Ciena, Colt Technology Services complete quantum-safe data transmission 
 
Colt Technology Services and Ciena have completed one of the fastest quantum-safe data transmissions ever demonstrated and the fastest across a transatlantic route. The companies said, "The landmark trial successfully protected live data running across 6900km of Colt's subsea and terrestrial network between New York and London with Ciena's WaveLogic 6 Extreme encryption solution. The trial proved that data can be securely transmitted at an 800Gb Ethernet service rate - fast enough to move data-centre-scale volumes across the Atlantic in seconds - while remaining protected against growing quantum threats. The trial is the latest in a series of quantum-safe encryption trials led by Colt across transatlantic subsea networks."

$CIEN Ciena price target raised to $490 from $405 at Morgan Stanley 
 
Morgan Stanley analyst Meta Marshall raised the firm's price target on Ciena to $490 from $405 and keeps an Equal Weight rating on the shares. UBS raised the firm's price target on Ciena (CIEN) to $508 from $285 and keeps a Neutral rating on the shares. $CIEN just printed $1.57B revenue with ~39.5% YoY growth and still managed to raise full year guidance to $6.3B. Corning shares fell sharply after comments from Hock E. Tan, CEO of Broadcom, who suggested the transition from copper to fiber within AI data-center racks may not occur in the near term. The remarks weighed on investor expectations for optical networking, a key growth driver tied to the AI boom. 
Corning previously partnered with Broadcom on co-packaged optics , a technology designed to connect AI chips using optical fiber instead of copper to improve power efficiency and processing capacity. However, Tan said direct-attached copper still offers lower latency, lower power consumption, and lower cost, making it the preferred option for rack-level connections for now. 
Corning stock dropped about 8%, marking its biggest single-day decline since April, despite having surged around 65% earlier this year on optimism around AI infrastructure demand.

Buying opportunities. 
Hock Tan’s exact comment was specifically about scale-up networking — within a rack or cluster domain — not scale-out (rack-to-rack, building-to-building). The Barron’s article framing conflates the two, which is a meaningful analytical error imo that likely amplified the selloff beyond what the comment warranted. Corning’s and Ciena’s primary near-term optical revenue opportunity is in scale-out — connecting racks and data centers — where fiber has already won and copper is not competitive at those distances. Tan may have deliberately used precise language knowing the market would misread it, buying Broadcom time on its own copper-heavy networking roadmap.

$AAOI vs. $LITE $COHR $CIEN  
AAOI’s Potential to Reach Peer Price Levels 
 
AAOI ($88) is far below peers ($250–$700), but share price depends on shares outstanding and growth. To reach similar levels, AAOI would need 3–8x upside, implying market cap expansion to $20–50B (matching peers). Possible if revenue scales as guided (e.g., to billions via AI optics), but unlikely short-term given losses and smaller base. Analysts see limited upside (targets <$80), suggesting it won’t match soon without outsized execution. Long-term potential exists in AI boom, but peers have stronger moats. 
 
Is AAOI’s $88 Price Justified? 
 
Likely a mix, leaning toward overhyped earnings/guidance. Fundamentals show losses and high P/S, but forward projections (e.g., revenue ramp) justify some premium. Recent surge (+768% from lows) aligns with AI sector froth, headlines emphasize “soaring outlook.” However, analyst targets below $88 and overbought technicals suggest bubble risk—price may correct if growth disappoints.

$AAOI vs. $LITE $COHR $CIEN  
52-Week Performance: All have surged on AI tailwinds—AAOI (+768% from low), LITE (+1,435%), COHR (+468%), CIEN (+609%)—but AAOI’s smaller base amplified gains. 
 
AAOI’s fundamentals don’t yet support peer-level scale or profitability, but its guidance signals potential catch-up if AI demand persists. Peers like LITE and CIEN also cite strong AI optics backlogs, but with better execution history.

$AAOI vs. $LITE $COHR $CIEN  
All four companies operate in the optical networking and photonics sector. 
  
AAOI is the smallest by market cap and revenue, with ongoing losses, while LITE, COHR, and CIEN are larger and profitable. AAOI’s recent earnings beat (Q4 2025: revenue $134M, EPS -$0.01 vs. expected -$0.11) and optimistic guidance (projecting rapid revenue scaling to ~$456M in 2025 and potentially $378M/month by 2027) have driven its price surge, but its fundamentals lag peers in profitability and scale. 
 
Market Cap: AAOI ($5.75B) << LITE ($50.05B), COHR ($48.55B), CIEN ($49.33B). AAOI would need massive growth to match peers’ size. 
 
Revenue (TTM): AAOI ($456M) << LITE ($2.11B), COHR ($6.29B), CIEN ($4.77B). AAOI’s growth outlook is aggressive, but peers have more established revenue bases. 
 
Net Income (TTM): AAOI (-$38M, loss) vs. LITE ($252M), COHR ($192M), CIEN ($123M). AAOI is unprofitable, while others generate positive income.

Ciena Corporation  unveiled the Vesta 200 6.4T CPX, a co-packaged optics (CPO) solution designed for AI data center applications. 
The product — Ciena’s first launch following its acquisition of Nubis Communications — is a pluggable CPO solution aimed at hyperscalers, cloud providers, and data center operators. The company said it can reduce power consumption by up to 70% versus traditional retimed optics. 
Vesta 200 6.4T CPX delivers ultra-high density through an in-house co-optimized design and 2D fiber interconnect technology. It supports 200G per lane deployments in space-constrained NICs, XPU servers, and switches up to 200 Tb/s. The solution features linear drive operation without retimers, supports up to 20 dB electrical loss from the host ASIC, and is built on an open, standards-based CPO ecosystem compatible with IEEE 802.3dj. 
Industry partners including Accton Technology and Samtec Inc.
